revision.comment: There is a lot of information on this guy it’s just I’m making this on iPad which honestly isn’t the smartest thing to do. I’m publishing it right now to save it. Also I don’t know why is name is also spelled as Takamimusuhi or Takamimusubi but I do know that the sources that spell his name differently are talking about the same god.
revision.text:
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese god of agriculture, who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It’s speculated that Takammusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" /> [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese god of agriculture, who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It’s speculated that Takammusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
== References == [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese god of agriculture, who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It’s speculated that Takammusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref> https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover </ref>
== References == [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese god of agriculture, who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It’s speculated that Takammusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref> https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover </ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined the god.<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>
== References == [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese [[god of agriculture]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>

revision.comment: I’m not 100% sure why some stories say he’s the father of Ame-no-oshihomimi but, many sources do say this.
revision.text:
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese [[god of agriculture]], who was the second of the first beings to come into
{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | children = [[Takuhadachiji-hime]] [[Omoikane]]
;Some versions, :[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] | name = Takamimusubi }}
existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It's speculated that Takammusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomim]]<nowiki/>i.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA181&dq=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjt8M-H8aDsAhVYa80KHbZIB1YQ6AEwA3oECAEQAg#v=onepage&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&f=false|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Takamimusubi+children&q=Takamimusubi+children&hl=en|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref>
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref>https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined the god.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]
{{Japan-myth-stub}}

== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref>https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined the god.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>

;Some versions, :[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] | name = Takamimusubi }} '''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, Lofty Growth) is a Japanese [[god of agriculture]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Takamimusuhi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/takamimusuhi/|access-date=2020-10-06|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Separate heavenly deities|url=https://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143|access-date=2020-10-06|website=Oxford Reference|language=en|doi=10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100455143}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA347&dq=Takamimusubi&hl=en|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>

A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA181&dq=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjt8M-H8aDsAhVYa80KHbZIB1YQ6AEwA3oECAEQAg#v=onepage&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&f=false|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Takamimusubi+children&q=Takamimusubi+children&hl=en|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref>https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined the god.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>

== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (some versions Takammusubi is the grandfather of Futodama) and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]. The [[Nihon Shoki]] says that he was the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA181&dq=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjt8M-H8aDsAhVYa80KHbZIB1YQ6AEwA3oECAEQAg#v=onepage&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&f=false|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Takamimusubi+children&q=Takamimusubi+children&hl=en|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA114&dq=takamimusubi&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Shinsen Shōjiroku]] he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to the god.<ref>https://www.google.com/books/edition/Japan_Encyclopedia/p2QnPijAEmEC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Takamimusubi&pg=PA413&printsec=frontcover</ref>

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[god of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (some versions Takammusubi is the grandfather of Futodama) and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]. According to [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']], he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|''Shinsen Shōjiroku'']], he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

"High Creator") is a [[god of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>

== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (some versions Takammusubi is the grandfather of Futodama) and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]. According to [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']], he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|''Shinsen Shōjiroku'']], he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and is a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']].
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>

According to [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|''Shinsen Shōjiroku'']], he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and is a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']].
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[god of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|language=en}}</ref>
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was the original god that the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]] claimed descent from.<ref name=":0" />
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (some versions Takammusubi is the grandfather of Futodama) and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]. According to [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']], he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|''Shinsen Shōjiroku'']], he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']].
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>

{{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[god of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the first beings to come into existence.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref>
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was the original [[tutelary deity]] for the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0" />
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[Takuhadachiji-hime]], [[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (some versions Takammusubi is the grandfather of Futodama) and some versions [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]. According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]]
{{Japan-myth-stub}}

{{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane (Shinto)|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] }}

It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[tutelary deity]] for the [[Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0" />

According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[Tamanoya]].<ref> http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 </ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]] is one of the shrines dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q125400]] Wikidata name: Takamimusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|qid=Q10939616}}; {{ill|Futodama|qid=Q11059539}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|qid=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|qid=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|qid=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|qid=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|qid=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|qid=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q125400]] Wikidata name: Takamimusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|WD=Q10939616}}; {{ill|Futodama|WD=Q11059539}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125400]] Wikidata name: Takamimusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|WD=Q10939616}}; {{ill|Futodama|WD=Q11059539}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|WD=Q10939616}}; {{ill|Futodama|WD=Q11059539}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Takamimusubi]][[en:Takamimusubi]][[es:Takami-musubi-no-kami]][[fa:تاکامیموسوبی]][[fr:Takamimusubi]][[id:Takamimusubi]][[ja:タカミムスヒ]][[ks:تاکامِمُسُبی]][[pl:Takamimusubi]][[ru:Такамимусуби-но ками]][[zh:高皇產靈尊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125400]] Wikidata name: Takamimusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None;
