{{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] for the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese imperial family]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

{{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

{{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>

The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}; {{ill|Futodama|WD=Q11059539|1=Futodama}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088|1=Omoikane}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205|1=Ame no Mikudaru}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832|1=Nunakawahime}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|1=Japanese mythology}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371|1=Kotoamatsukami}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese god of agriculture}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takamimusubi | image = File:Takamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 高御産巣日神 | parents = None; self-generated | children = *[[Takuhadachiji-hime]] *[[Omoikane|Omoikane]] *[[Futodama]] | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | offspring = {{ill|Amanōshihi|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}; {{ill|Futodama|WD=Q11059539|1=Futodama}}; {{ill|Mihotsuhime|WD=Q6378698|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Omoikane|WD=Q586088|1=Omoikane}}; {{ill|Q110120205|WD=Q110120205|1=Ame no Mikudaru}}; {{ill|Q97184246|WD=Q97184246|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}} | relatives = {{ill|Nunakawa hime|WD=Q11142832|1=Nunakawahime}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|1=Japanese mythology}} | member_of = {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|WD=Q776371|1=Kotoamatsukami}} }}
'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Musubi]]

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>

'''Takamimusubi''' (高御産巣日神, lit. "High Creator") is a [[:en:God of agriculture|God of agriculture]] in [[Japanese mythology]], who was the second of the [[Kotoamatsukami|first beings to come into existence]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Leeming|first=David|title=The Oxford Companion to World Mythology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|isbn=9780195156690}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
It is speculated that Takamimusubi was originally the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} for the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Japanese imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Takamimusubi&pg=PA347|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall|first2=John Whitney|last3=Brown|first3=Delmer Myers|last4=Press|first4=Cambridge University|last5=Jansen|first5=Marius B.|last6=McCullough|first6=William H.|last7=Shively|first7=Donald H.|last8=Yamamura|first8=Kozo|last9=Duus|first9=Peter|date=1988|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=347|language=en}}</ref> According to the [[Kojiki]], Takamimusubi was a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9951|archive-url=|access-date=2021-10-05|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mythology == According to ''[[Kojiki]]'', when the [[Japanese creation myth|heaven and earth were created]], [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was the first one to appear in [[Takamagahara]], Takamimusubi the second, and [[Kamimusubi]] the third.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Tobe|first=Tamio|title=Yaoyorozu no kamigami Nihon no shinrei-tachi no purofīru『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』|publisher=Shinkigensha|year=1997|isbn=9784883172993|location=Japan}}</ref>
One myth tells of a bird named Nakime who was sent down to earth to check in on [[Ame no Wakahiko|Amewakahiko]]. Amewakahiko shot the bird with his bow. The arrow pierced through the bird, but the arrow flew all the way to heaven. Takamimusubi saw the arrow and threw it back at the earth where it hit Amewakahiko while he was laying in bed, killing him.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ame-no-wakahiko&pg=PA6|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2024}}
== Family == He is the father of several gods including [[:ja:栲幡千千姫命|Takuhadachiji-hime]] (栲幡千千姫), [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], [[Futodama]] (in some versions Takamimusubi is instead the grandfather of Futodama)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52|access-date=2020-11-07|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> and some versions {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jqfZAAAAMAAJ&q=Takamimusubi+children|title=Watarai Shintô: An Intellectual History of the Outer Shrine in Ise|date=1996|publisher=Research School CNWS|isbn=978-90-73782-79-2|pages=46|language=en}}</ref> According to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the father of [[Sukunabikona]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=R. A. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=Takamimusubi-No-Kami&pg=PA181|title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=takamimusubi&pg=PA114|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref>
According to ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', he is the grandfather of [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
In one version of the Nihon Shoki, [[:ja:三穂津姫|Mihotsuhime]] (三穂津姫) is the daughter of Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9364|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
He is the grandfather of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who descended on [[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]] first as a member of the Imperial Family and was a grandson of [[Amaterasu]], according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2021}} {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== Worship == [[Izumo-taisha]], one of the oldest Shinto shrines, is dedicated to Takamimusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|title=Japan encyclopedia|publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=9780674017535|pages=413}}</ref> [[Towatari Shrine]] was converted into a Shinto shrine in the 19th century, and now enshrines several important Shinto creator deities, including Takamimusubi.<ref name="shrine website">{{cite web |title=登渡神社について |url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya |website=Towatari Shrine Official Website |language=Japanese|access-date=24 March 2023}}</ref>
Hasshinden was once a temple that enshrined him.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1298 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University }}</ref>
